execution_context.failed_step: Inspect `scripts/lib.sh` and `agents/orchestrator.sh` to locate the single task-registry update path used when a task run finishes, and list the exact failure fields already available at that point (`run_id`, `result`, `attempts`, `provider`, `failed_step`, timestamps, score, duration, branch`).
plan_steps: Inspect `scripts/lib.sh` and `agents/orchestrator.sh` to locate the single task-registry update path used when a task run finishes, and list the exact failure fields already available at that point (`run_id`, `result`, `attempts`, `provider`, `failed_step`, timestamps, score, duration, branch`).

Extend that existing registry-write helper in `scripts/lib.sh` so failed tasks persist a deterministic `failure_context` object in `codex-memory/tasks.json`, reusing existing execution metadata and adding the structured failed-step details without creating any new storage file or changing success-path behavior.

Update `agents/orchestrator.sh` to pass the missing structured failure values into the existing registry update call when `RESULT=FAILURE`, ensuring empty values are normalized consistently and retries remain capped at 2.

Add or adjust a small regression check that exercises a failed task update and verifies `codex-memory/tasks.json` contains the expected `failure_context` fields for the task while successful task writes remain unchanged.

Run the targeted verification for the registry update path and a simple manual inspection of the resulting `codex-memory/tasks.json` entry to confirm the new failure context is persisted deterministically and does not break the current execution flow.

failure_context.failed_step: Inspect `scripts/lib.sh` and `agents/orchestrator.sh` to locate the single task-registry update path used when a task run finishes, and list the exact failure fields already available at that point (`run_id`, `result`, `attempts`, `provider`, `failed_step`, timestamps, score, duration, branch`).

execution_context.failed_step: Inspect the strategy follow-up creation path in `codex-dashboard/server.js` and the shared task-registry helpers in `scripts/lib.sh` to identify the single code path that creates follow-up tasks from a failed task, plus the exact existing task fields used for parent/root linkage.
plan_steps: Inspect the strategy follow-up creation path in `codex-dashboard/server.js` and the shared task-registry helpers in `scripts/lib.sh` to identify the single code path that creates follow-up tasks from a failed task, plus the exact existing task fields used for parent/root linkage.

Update that follow-up task creation path so each new strategy follow-up task copies the source task's `original_failed_root_id` when present, otherwise initializes it from the failed source task's own id, without changing unrelated task fields or execution behavior.

Update any task normalization or persistence helper that rewrites task records to preserve `original_failed_root_id` unchanged for existing tasks and newly written follow-up tasks, avoiding empty-string regressions during save/load cycles.

Verify the change with a focused end-to-end check: create or simulate a failed task, trigger strategy follow-up generation, confirm the follow-up task record stores the expected `original_failed_root_id`, and confirm repeated follow-ups keep the same root id rather than replacing it.

failure_context.failed_step: Inspect the strategy follow-up creation path in `codex-dashboard/server.js` and the shared task-registry helpers in `scripts/lib.sh` to identify the single code path that creates follow-up tasks from a failed task, plus the exact existing task fields used for parent/root linkage.

execution_context.failed_step: Inspect `codex-dashboard/index.html` and `codex-dashboard/server.js` to identify the current dashboard panel structure, shared style tokens, and existing task/metrics payload fields that can support new security, audit, and governance panels without changing existing behavior.
plan_steps: Inspect `codex-dashboard/index.html` and `codex-dashboard/server.js` to identify the current dashboard panel structure, shared style tokens, and existing task/metrics payload fields that can support new security, audit, and governance panels without changing existing behavior.

Extend `codex-dashboard/server.js` to expose a minimal, deterministic dashboard payload for enterprise panels by deriving counts and recent records from existing task registry, metrics, and history data paths instead of adding a new persistence store.

Add three bounded dashboard panels in `codex-dashboard/index.html` for security, audit, and governance using the existing layout system and responsive breakpoints, wiring them only to the new server payload and preserving current task board interactions.

Update or add a focused smoke test fixture in `tests/system-smoke.sh` that exercises the new panel data and rendering path with stable sample records so the change remains deterministic and debuggable.

Run the relevant verification commands and manually check the dashboard layout at mobile and desktop widths to confirm the new panels render correctly and that existing dashboard flows remain unchanged.

failure_context.failed_step: Inspect `codex-dashboard/index.html` and `codex-dashboard/server.js` to identify the current dashboard panel structure, shared style tokens, and existing task/metrics payload fields that can support new security, audit, and governance panels without changing existing behavior.

tasks.title: Persist dashboard task intent metadata before queue handoff
tasks.reason: Task task-033-redesign-the-dashboard-into-an-enterpris failed after the system tried to reshape raw dashboard task text too late in the flow. A narrower step is to persist intent metadata at task creation before approval or queue handoff changes.
tasks.hypothesis: If dashboard-created backlog items store deterministic intent metadata when they enter tasks.json, later approval and execution steps can consume stable context without rewriting the raw task text in multiple places.
tasks.experiment: Store a task_intent object when dashboard backlog items are created, containing submitter-facing objective, target area, and fixed safety constraints, without changing queue execution yet.
success_criteria: New dashboard-created pending tasks persist a task_intent object with deterministic keys.
Existing approval, editing, and queue behavior remain unchanged for tasks without task_intent.
The dashboard API returns task_intent for newly created pending tasks.
A deterministic test proves the same dashboard request creates the same task_intent payload on every run.
tasks.rollback: Remove the task_intent write path and API exposure while leaving the rest of task creation unchanged.
tasks.source_task_title: Redesign the dashboard into an enterprise-grade responsive console for iPhone, iPad, and desktop

execution_context.failed_step: Inspect the approval-to-queue handoff in `codex-dashboard/server.js` and identify the single function/path where an approved dashboard task is turned into `queue_handoff`, listing the exact `task_intent` fields available immediately before and after that conversion.
plan_steps: Inspect the approval-to-queue handoff in `codex-dashboard/server.js` and identify the single function/path where an approved dashboard task is turned into `queue_handoff`, listing the exact `task_intent` fields available immediately before and after that conversion.

Modify that existing handoff path in `codex-dashboard/server.js` so the current `task_intent` object is preserved deterministically when `queue_handoff` is created, reusing the existing task record shape and avoiding any new storage path or broader refactor.

Update the task normalization/serialization logic in `codex-dashboard/server.js` so persisted tasks continue to emit the preserved `task_intent` fields after handoff, with stable defaults for missing string and list values.

Add or adjust a focused regression check in `tests/system-smoke.sh` or the nearest existing dashboard smoke fixture to cover an approved task moving to `queue_handoff` and verify the expected `task_intent` fields remain present in `codex-memory/tasks.json`.

Run the targeted smoke or fixture verification for the approval handoff path and inspect the resulting task entry to confirm `task_intent` survives queue handoff without changing unrelated approval or execution behavior.

tasks.title: Inspect `scripts/lib.sh` and `agents/orchestrator.sh` to locate the single task-registry update path used when a task run finishes, and list
tasks.reason: Task task-023-persist-structured-failure-context-for-s failed while still spanning too much scope. The narrowest deterministic next step is to complete only the first failed plan step before retrying any broader work.
tasks.hypothesis: If the next run executes only the first failed step from the broader task, the system will recover faster than repeating the full multi-step task at the same size.
tasks.experiment: Execute only this bounded child step next: Inspect `scripts/lib.sh` and `agents/orchestrator.sh` to locate the single task-registry update path used when a task run finishes, and list the exact failure fields already available at that point (`run_id`, `result`, `attempts`, `provider`, `failed_step`, timestamps, score, duration, branch`). Do not implement later plan steps from the parent task in the same run.
success_criteria: The child task changes only the code needed for this single failed step.
The parent task is not retried as a whole in the same run.
Verification covers the exact failed path named in the child step.
Execution context records that this child task came from a broader failed parent task.
tasks.rollback: Discard the child-task split and return to the previous whole-task retry behavior.
tasks.source_task_title: Persist structured failure context for strategy follow-ups

tasks.title: Tighten iPhone task card density and typography so the board reads like an enterprise console without changing task actions
tasks.reason: Seed learned provider routing with a bounded Claude-owned mobile UI task. This isolates card spacing, typography, and hierarchy on iPhone widths so the system can gather comparable provider success data from a real production-facing dashboard change.
tasks.hypothesis: Claude can complete a narrow presentation-only mobile board refinement with higher reliability than the broader multi-panel dashboard tasks that have been failing.
tasks.experiment: Refine only the visual density and hierarchy of task cards on iPhone widths in the dashboard. Improve spacing, typography scale, metadata grouping, and touch-friendly rhythm while preserving the existing actions, data fields, and backend behavior.
success_criteria: Task cards are easier to scan on iPhone widths without introducing layout overlap or clipped actions.
The change stays presentation-only and does not alter task state transitions or API payloads.
Verification covers the mobile board rendering path specifically.
tasks.rollback: Revert the dashboard card styling changes and keep the previous mobile card layout.

task_intent.objective: Improve iPhone task-card readability and density without changing behavior.
task_intent.context_hint: The board should feel like a secure enterprise console rather than a basic page on narrow screens.
constraints: Limit the change to dashboard presentation code.
Do not change task actions, approval behavior, or API schemas.
Keep the layout usable on existing iPhone widths.
success_signals: Spacing is tighter but still touch-safe.
No existing controls disappear or overlap.

provider_selection.reason: Pinned to Claude to build comparable provider history from a small UI-only task.

history.queue_task: Tighten iPhone task card density and typography so the board reads like an enterprise console without changing task actions
history.note: Task was added manually as a bounded Claude learning-seed task for mobile UI quality.

tasks.title: Add a compact live activity strip for running work that shows task title, provider, lane, and current step in the dashboard
tasks.reason: Seed learned provider routing with a bounded Claude-owned observability task. This directly improves interactive transparency in the UI by showing what is running and who is working on it, while remaining small enough to produce useful provider performance data.
tasks.hypothesis: Claude can reliably deliver a small live-work visibility component for the existing dashboard when the scope is limited to a compact summary strip.
tasks.experiment: Add a compact dashboard strip that summarizes the currently running work using already available status data. Show the active task, provider, queue lane, and current step or note without redesigning the whole board.
success_criteria: The dashboard surfaces the currently running task and responsible provider clearly.
The component works on mobile and tablet widths without breaking the existing board layout.
The implementation reuses existing status data instead of introducing a broad new backend workflow.
tasks.rollback: Remove the live activity strip and restore the previous dashboard layout.

task_intent.objective: Expose live work progress, active provider, and lane in a small dashboard component.
task_intent.context_hint: The user wants to see interactively what is being worked on and who is working on it.
constraints: Keep the scope to one compact strip or panel.
Do not redesign the whole dashboard in the same run.
success_signals: Active task, provider, and lane are visible in the UI.
The strip remains readable on iPhone and iPad.
The change is incremental and auditable.

provider_selection.reason: Pinned to Claude to gather outcome data on a narrow UI observability change.

history.queue_task: Add a compact live activity strip for running work that shows task title, provider, lane, and current step in the dashboard
history.note: Task was added manually as a bounded Claude learning-seed task for live work visibility.

tasks.title: Refine iPad board layout into a stable two-column enterprise view with a pinned system status summary
tasks.reason: Seed learned provider routing with a bounded Claude-owned tablet UI task. This creates a second real mobile-class comparison point without repeating the broad failed dashboard redesign scope.
tasks.hypothesis: Claude can complete a focused tablet layout refinement more reliably than a full responsive dashboard overhaul when the scope is constrained to structure and status placement.
tasks.experiment: Refine only the iPad and tablet board layout to use a stable two-column arrangement with a pinned system-status summary. Preserve the existing data model and controls while improving the information hierarchy for medium-width screens.
success_criteria: Tablet widths present a stable two-column hierarchy without broken wrapping.
System status remains visible without hiding core task controls.
The change is limited to layout structure and presentation.
tasks.rollback: Revert the tablet layout changes and restore the previous responsive behavior.

task_intent.objective: Improve the tablet dashboard layout and persistent status visibility.
task_intent.context_hint: The iPad view should feel structured and deliberate instead of collapsing into a stretched phone layout.
constraints: Do not change task APIs or queue semantics.
Keep the change focused on layout and status placement.
Maintain compatibility with the existing desktop and mobile views.
success_signals: System status stays visible while scanning tasks.
No controls regress on desktop or phone.

provider_selection.reason: Pinned to Claude to add a second bounded responsive-layout sample to provider history.

history.queue_task: Refine iPad board layout into a stable two-column enterprise view with a pinned system status summary
history.note: Task was added manually as a bounded Claude learning-seed task for tablet dashboard layout quality.
